preamble: The transformation from an alveolar flap to a voiced alveolar stop is a process of fortition or stopping, where the brief contact of the flap is lengthened into a full closure.

examples.note: Children often substitute the flap allophone with a full voiced stop during phonological development.
Intervocalic stops are often maintained or strengthened in certain lexical items where other dialects might use a flap.

sources: Smith, N. V. (1973). The Acquisition of Phonology.
Green, L. J. (2002). African American English: A Linguistic Introduction.
